title: Strategy-Formulation Matrices (SWOT, SPACE, BCG, IE, QSPM)
simple_html:
After you have studied the outside world and looked honestly inside your own company, you still face the hardest question: which strategies should we actually pursue? Strategy-formulation matrices are simple tables and grids that turn all your analysis into a short list of recommended moves. They take messy information and force it into a clear, comparable shape so a leadership team can decide together.

The five workhorse tools

  • SWOT (TOWS): matches internal Strengths and Weaknesses against external Opportunities and Threats to generate four kinds of action ideas.
  • SPACE: plots your firm on two axes (financial vs. competitive strength, and industry vs. environmental stability) to suggest an overall posture: aggressive, conservative, defensive, or competitive.
  • BCG: sorts your products or business units into Stars, Cash Cows, Question Marks, and Dogs based on market growth and market share.
  • IE (Internal-External): places business units on a 3x3 grid using your IFE and EFE scores to say whether to grow, hold, or harvest.
  • QSPM: the only tool that scores and ranks competing strategies numerically, so you can pick objectively.

Why it matters

These tools move a team from opinion ("I just feel we should expand") to evidence ("three matrices point to growth, and the QSPM ranks market development highest"). They are heavily tested on MBA exams because they connect the whole strategy course together.

Key takeaways

  • Matrices are a decision pipeline: input stage (IFE/EFE/CPM) feeds the matching stage (SWOT, SPACE, BCG, IE, Grand Strategy), which feeds the decision stage (QSPM).
  • Each tool answers a different question; use several and look for convergence.
  • The matrices help you think; they do not replace judgment.
  • QSPM is unique because it gives a numerical ranking of alternative strategies.
intermediate_html:
Strategy-formulation matrices are the analytical engine of the strategic-management process. David's three-stage framework organizes them: an Input Stage, a Matching Stage, and a Decision Stage. Understanding which tool sits where is the key to using them correctly and to scoring well on exams.

Stage 1 - Input

The input stage summarizes the basic information needed to formulate strategy: the IFE Matrix (internal factors), the EFE Matrix (external factors), and the CPM (competitive profile). These produce weighted scores you reuse in later stages.

Stage 2 - Matching

The matching stage generates feasible alternatives by aligning internal and external factors.

  • SWOT/TOWS: builds four strategy sets - SO (use strengths to seize opportunities), WO (fix weaknesses by using opportunities), ST (use strengths to avoid threats), WT (defensive, minimize weaknesses and threats).
  • SPACE Matrix: two internal dimensions (Financial Strength FS, Competitive Advantage CA) and two external (Environmental Stability ES, Industry Strength IS). You average the ratings, plot a directional vector, and read off the posture quadrant: Aggressive, Conservative, Defensive, or Competitive.
  • BCG Matrix: relative market share (x) vs. industry growth (y) classifies units as Stars, Cash Cows, Question Marks, or Dogs, each with a typical strategy (build, hold, harvest, divest).
  • IE Matrix: a 3x3 grid plotting IFE (x) against EFE (y). Cells I, II, IV = "grow and build"; III, V, VII = "hold and maintain"; VI, VIII, IX = "harvest or divest."
  • Grand Strategy Matrix: classifies the firm by market growth and competitive position into four quadrants.

Stage 3 - Decision

The QSPM (Quantitative Strategic Planning Matrix) is the only technique designed to determine the relative attractiveness of feasible alternative actions. You list key factors and weights (from IFE/EFE), then assign an Attractiveness Score (AS, 1-4) to each strategy for each factor, multiply by the weight to get a Total Attractiveness Score (TAS), and sum. The strategy with the highest sum-TAS is most attractive.

Worked mini-example

Imagine a regional coffee chain. Its IFE = 2.8 and EFE = 3.1, so on the IE Matrix it lands in cell II - "grow and build." SPACE shows strong FS and IS but a competitive CA, producing an aggressive vector. SWOT matching surfaces two SO strategies: (A) market development into a neighboring city, and (B) product development (premium cold-brew line). To choose, build a QSPM. Suppose for the factor "rising premium-coffee demand" (weight 0.15), market development earns AS=3 (TAS 0.45) while product development earns AS=4 (TAS 0.60). Repeat across all factors and sum. If product development totals 5.4 vs. market development's 4.9, you recommend product development first.

How to apply it

  1. Run the input matrices once; reuse their weights and scores everywhere.
  2. Use at least two matching tools and look for convergent signals.
  3. Translate convergent quadrants into 2-4 specific candidate strategies.
  4. Use the QSPM to rank them; never compute it without the input-stage weights.
  5. Apply judgment: a high TAS still needs resource and cultural feasibility.

Exam tip: examiners love asking you to state which stage a tool belongs to and to interpret an IE cell or a SPACE quadrant - memorize the cell-to-action mapping.
full_html:
The strategy-formulation matrices constitute the most operationally tested portion of a strategic-management course. They embody a deceptively simple proposition: that the formulation of strategy can be made systematic, transparent, and partly quantitative without surrendering managerial judgment. The dominant pedagogical scaffolding is Fred David's analytical-framework model, which arranges the principal techniques into three sequential stages (David, 2017). This lesson treats each stage rigorously, links the tools to their intellectual origins, and addresses the recurring errors that depress both decision quality and exam marks.

The three-stage analytical framework

The framework is a funnel. The Input Stage codifies raw situational data into weighted scores. The Matching Stage creates feasible alternative strategies by aligning internal and external factors. The Decision Stage uses a single tool, the QSPM, to prioritize among those alternatives. The conceptual elegance is that information flows forward: weights and ratings produced once at the input stage are reused, ensuring internal consistency.

Stage 1 - the input matrices

The IFE (Internal Factor Evaluation) Matrix distills the internal audit; the EFE (External Factor Evaluation) Matrix distills the external audit; the CPM (Competitive Profile Matrix) benchmarks the firm against rivals on critical success factors. Each uses the same arithmetic: assign weights summing to 1.0 reflecting relative importance, rate each factor (typically 1-4), and compute weighted scores. These matrices descend from the SWOT tradition and the structured environmental-scanning literature, and they presuppose the external- and internal-analysis lessons that precede this one (see Lessons 2 and 3).

Stage 2 - the matching matrices

SWOT / TOWS Matrix. The SWOT concept is usually traced to the Harvard policy tradition and Andrews's articulation of matching internal capability to external opportunity (Andrews, 1971), with the LCAG/Harvard model providing the underlying logic. Weihrich (1982) formalized the TOWS matrix to force matching rather than mere listing, generating SO, WO, ST, and WT strategy sets. The central discipline is the cross-pairing of one internal with one external factor to produce an actionable strategy.

SPACE (Strategic Position and Action Evaluation) Matrix. Developed by Rowe, Mason, and Dickel (1982), SPACE uses two internal dimensions - Financial Strength (FS) and Competitive Advantage (CA) - and two external dimensions - Environmental Stability (ES) and Industry Strength (IS). After rating and averaging each dimension, the analyst plots the resultant directional vector to identify one of four postures: Aggressive (strong industry, strong finances), Conservative (cash-rich but weak industry), Competitive (attractive industry but financial weakness), or Defensive (unattractive industry and weak finances). SPACE's contribution is to integrate financial and competitive logic into a single recommended stance.

BCG Growth-Share Matrix. Created by the Boston Consulting Group under Bruce Henderson (Henderson, 1970), the BCG matrix plots relative market share against market growth rate, yielding Stars, Cash Cows, Question Marks, and Dogs. Its theoretical engines are the experience curve and the product life cycle; its prescriptive logic is portfolio cash balance - milk Cash Cows to fund Stars and selected Question Marks while divesting Dogs.

IE (Internal-External) Matrix. A 3x3 grid plotting the weighted IFE score on the x-axis and the EFE score on the y-axis. It is more granular than BCG because it uses nine cells and incorporates more variables. The standard mapping: cells I, II, and IV imply grow and build (intensive or integrative strategies); cells III, V, and VII imply hold and maintain (market penetration, product development); cells VI, VIII, and IX imply harvest or divest.

Grand Strategy Matrix. Positions the firm by market (rapid vs. slow growth) and competitive position (strong vs. weak), generating quadrant-specific strategy menus. It is a useful triangulation tool alongside SPACE and IE.

Stage 3 - the QSPM

The Quantitative Strategic Planning Matrix is the only analytical technique designed to determine the relative attractiveness of feasible alternative strategies (David, 2017). Procedure: (1) list the key external and internal factors from the input stage with their weights; (2) examine the matching-stage strategies and select alternatives to compare; (3) assign an Attractiveness Score (AS, 1-4) indicating how much each factor affects the choice between strategies - leaving AS blank when a factor has no differential bearing; (4) compute the Total Attractiveness Score (TAS) = weight x AS; (5) sum the TAS for each strategy. The higher sum signals greater relative attractiveness.

Worked calculation. Comparing Strategy A (vertical integration) and Strategy B (joint venture) on the factor "supplier bargaining power" (weight 0.10): if vertical integration neutralizes that force, AS_A = 4 (TAS 0.40) and AS_B = 2 (TAS 0.20). Aggregating across, say, twenty factors might yield sum-TAS of 5.95 for A versus 5.10 for B, indicating vertical integration is the more attractive strategy given the firm's specific factor profile.

Nuances, debates, and common mistakes

  • Spurious precision. Critics note that quantitative outputs (especially QSPM and BCG) can mask subjective inputs; the numbers are only as good as the underlying ratings (Mintzberg, 1994, cautions against over-formalized "strategic planning").
  • BCG's single-variable trap. Market share is an imperfect proxy for competitive strength, and growth for attractiveness; the GE-McKinsey nine-cell matrix was developed precisely to add multifactor richness.
  • Listing instead of matching. The most common SWOT error is producing four lists with no cross-paired strategies - defeating the entire purpose Weihrich (1982) identified.
  • Ignoring convergence. No single matrix is decisive; rigor lies in triangulating IE, SPACE, BCG, and Grand Strategy and explaining divergences.
  • Leaving QSPM weights inconsistent. Weights must be carried unchanged from IFE/EFE; re-inventing them breaks the framework's logic.
  • Treating output as mandate. Matrices inform; implementation feasibility, culture, and governance (see Lessons 7 and 8) determine whether a high-TAS strategy can actually be executed.

Mastery means knowing not just how to compute each matrix but where it sits in the funnel, what intellectual lineage it carries, and how to defend a recommendation by showing convergence across tools and a transparent QSPM ranking.
